Defendants' motion to compel further responses and compliance is granted in small part. Plaintiff Arkday Berger need not provide any further responses to the discovery seeking information about his payment of bills sent to him by any attorneys other than defendants because that information is not relevant nor likely to lead to the discovery of any admissible evidence. Re-Notice Of Defendants Motion For Summary Judgment And Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Jul-16-2018 Denied
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Paul Frassetto, Esq. 415-354-2700 for Plaintiff Arkady Berger.
Ronald Losch, Esq. 415-956-8400 of Losch & Ehrlich for Defendant LOSCH & EHRLICH, RONALD LOSCH, MARK MEYER.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court shares his views. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT LOSCH & EHRLICH, RONALD LOSCH, MARK MEYER'S Motion For Summary Judgment And Summary Adjudication.

Defendants Losch & Ehrlich, Ronald Losch, and Mark Meyer's motion for summary judgement and their alternative motion for summary adjudication are denied in their entirety. There is a triable dispute whether plaintiff Arkady Berger sustained any contract or tort based on (1) the statement in Mr. Berger's declaration that he was not fully credited for sanction awards resulting in his being "under-credited" by approximately $4,500; and (2) defendants did not distribute to Mr. Berger the portion of the interest on the attorney's fees collected that accrued on fees that had been paid by him. There is also a triable dispute whether Mr. Berger gave his informed consent to the right of defendants to collect interest on overdue fees billed to him and thus whether he was damaged in the amount of the interest he was charged. Hernandez v. Siegel (2014) 230 Cal.App.4th 165, 175 is distinguishable from this case. The parties' fee agreement did not provide that no fees will be due until a favorable judgment or settlement is reached, the fees are not statutory, and the policy rationale that "it would make little sense to award interest on a fee award to anyone other than the attorney whose labor remains uncompensated" does not apply when a client pays the attorney on a continuous basis before a fees award is made. While interest on fees awarded in excess of fees that the client has already paid belongs to the attorney, absent a contractual agreement to the contrary, there is triable issue whether Mr. Berger was paid the interests on fees he had already paid. There is also a triable issue whether Mr. Berger gave informed consent to be charged interest on overdue bills given the absence of an interest provision in the fee agreement. Based on defendants' repeated assurances about the accuracy of all calculations and compliance with Mr. Berger's directions as to the trust account, a reasonable juror could infer that defendants had fraudulent intent to support the fraudulent inducement claim. There is also a triable issue whether Mr. Berger had full knowledge of the facts on which his fraud allegations are based. Mr. Berger's statement in his declaration that it was not till May and June 2016 that he became aware that defendants were dishonest in their billing practices precludes summary adjudication on the rescission claim for rescission based on the defense of waiver. Mr. Berger's evidence concerning Mr. Meyer's representations about the judgement, sanctions and interest calculations and his communications regarding the distribution of the funds in the trust account creates a triable issue as to Mr. Meyer's personal liability for fraud, breach of fiduciary duty and conversion. (Shafer v. Berger, Kahn, Shafton, Moss, Figler, Simon & Gladstone (2003) 107 Cal.App.4th

Counsel for Mr. Berger is required to prepare a proposed order that complies with CCP 437c(g) and CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: M.Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR #8600, torreanom@aol.com, Reported. =(302/HK)

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Nov-30-2017 Sustained
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Jennifer McGlone, Esq. of Holland Law, Tel. 415.200.4983, for cross defendant/ moving party;
No other appearance.

RULING: There being no objection, the Court adopts the tentative ruling. CROSS-DEFENDANT ARKADY BERGER'S DEMURRER TO FIRST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT, as to the second and fourth (erroneously labeled third) causes of action in the first amended cross-complaint filed by cross-complainants Ronald Losch and Losch & Ehrlich is SUSTAINED without leave to amend as to both causes of action. The second cause of action for breach of the "McHugh" contract fails because Mr. Berger has exercised his right to void that contract and thus the Losch & Ehrlich parties are limited to quantum meruit recovery, which they have alleged in their third cause of action. The fourth cause of action for fraud fails because the alleged reliance was the inability to obtain different compensation terms for the Losch & Ehrlich parties' representation of Mr. Berger and the uncompensated expenditure of time by the Losch & Ehrlich parties but there is no allegation that Mr. Berger would have agreed to different compensation terms and the allegations of the first amended cross-complaint show otherwise.

ORDER SIGNED. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: G. Gonzales; Not reported.

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Oct-02-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Jennifer McGlone of the law offices of Holland Law (415-200-4983) appears as counsel for plaintiff/opposing party

No other appearance is made in this matter

The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANT MARK MEYER's DEMURRER TO FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT, as to the first, second, sixth, and seventh causes of action in the first amended complaint filed by plaintiff Berger for breach of contract, breach of implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, fraudulent inducement and rescission is SUSTAINED without leave to amend. The contract causes of action do not pertain to Mr. Meyer because he was not a party to the contract. Defendant Meyer's demurrer to the third, fourth, and fifth causes of action in the first amended complaint filed by plaintiff Berger for breach of fiduciary duties, fraud and conversion is OVERRULED. As to the third cause of action, plaintiff has alleged sufficient facts arguing that the Mr. Meyers had a fiduciary duty to Mr. Berger and that he personally breached his duty by overbilling for services rendered, failing to communicate with Mr. Berger as his attorney, falsely reassuring Mr. Berger that the defendants placed disputed funds in a third party client trust account, and withdrawing money from the trust account without Mr. Berger's authorization. As to the fourth cause of action, plaintiff has alleged fraud with specificity to survive the demurrer. As to the fifth cause of action, plaintiff has alleged sufficient facts arguing that Mr. Berger was entitled to the money that the defendants, including Mr. Meyer, withdrew from the client trust account without Mr. Berger's authorization. Written order is signed and filed in open court.
